chunks.page_summary: This page provides a technical guide on integrating Rheonics sensors with an RS485-LN LoRaWAN converter and SenseCAP M2 gateway to enable remote data visualization through The Things Stack.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Using RS485-LN LoRaWAN Converter for Rheonics Sensors Remote Data Visualization
Modified on: Fri, 14 Nov, 2025 at 11:00 PM

What products are involved?
This article is based on using Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME), SenseCAP M2 Gateway, and the RS485 to LoRaWAN Converter.

What is the purpose of this article?
This article describes the process of integrating Rheonics sensors with the RS485-LN converter and the SenseCAP M2, so data can be transmitted wirelessly and visualized remotely.

1. Overview
LoRaWAN is a long-range, low-power communication protocol designed for transmitting sensor data over wide areas. In this article, the RS485-LN converts data from the SME via Modbus RTU (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217005) and converts it into LoRaWAN messages. These messages are then sent to the SenseCAP M2 gateway, which is connected to the Internet and sends the data to The Things Network (TTN) or other LoRaWAN network servers. TTN is a LoRaWAN ecosystem that includes The Things Stack (TTS), a LoRaWAN network server that enables remote data access. Once received, the data can be decoded and can be visualized remotely. Following this setup, Rheonics sensors can transmit sensor values wirelessly and be monitored in real time from anywhere through The Things Stack server.

Figure 1: Architecture of Rheonics SME, RS485-LN, SenseCAP M2, and The Things Stack integration

To configure the SenseCAP M2 LoRaWAN gateway refer to the following article Configuring LoRaWAN Gateway for Remote Data Visualization of Rheonics Sensors (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000419454).

2. RS485-LN Configuration
The RS485-LN configuration needs to be done in The Things Stack (TTS) and using the Dragino RS485 Configure Tool.

2.1. Configuration in The Things Stack (TTS)
1. Enter (https://console.cloud.thethings.network/) The Things Network Console (https://console.cloud.thethings.network/) and access the account.
2. Select Create application
Figure 2: Application creation
3. Add a name and ID for the new application
key_facts: The RS485-LN LoRaWAN converter is used for remote data visualization of Rheonics sensors.
The article describes the integration process of Rheonics sensors with the RS485-LN converter and SenseCAP M2 gateway for wireless data transmission and remote visualization.
LoRaWAN is a long-range, low-power communication protocol designed for transmitting sensor data over wide areas.
The RS485-LN converts data from Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME) via Modbus RTU into LoRaWAN messages.
The SenseCAP M2 gateway connects to the Internet and sends LoRaWAN messages to The Things Network (TTN) or other LoRaWAN network servers.
The Things Stack (TTS) is a LoRaWAN network server within the TTN ecosystem that facilitates remote data access and visualization.
After setup, Rheonics sensors can transmit values wirelessly and be monitored in real time through The Things Stack server.
RS485-LN configuration involves both The Things Stack (TTS) and the Dragino RS485 Configure Tool.

New API key created for gateway
warnings_notes: To configure the SenseCAP M2 LoRaWAN gateway refer to the following article Configuring LoRaWAN Gateway for Remote Data Visualization of Rheonics Sensors (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000419454).

5. Register the end device with the following parameters
*   Frequency plan: Select a Frequency plan that is the same as the one specified in the device and the SenseCap M2 so it is compatible. In this case, Europe 863-870 MHz (SF9 for RX2 - recommended)
*   LoRaWAN version: LoRaWAN Specification 1.0.3
*   Additional LoRaWAN class capabilities: Class C (Continuous)

Register end device

Network defaults
  Use network's default MAC settings

Figure 5: End device configuration

6. In the Provisioning information section, fill with the following and click on Register end device.
*   JoinEUI: e.g. 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 - This identifies the Join Server, which handles the initial communication between the end device and TTS. It is usually assigned by the device manufacturer, but it can also be set by the user. If no JoinEUI is provided, a value of all zeros can be used if the Join Server supports it. It is possible to use the same JoinEUI for different end devices, but the DevEUI should be unique per end device.
*   DevEUI: from RS485-LN
*   AppKey: generate a random key (necessary for the RS485-LN to be able to connect to TTS)
*   End device ID: e.g. RS485-converter
key_facts: End devices within an application can be registered and managed.
The frequency plan selected for the end device must be compatible with the device itself and the SenseCap M2.
The LoRaWAN Specification 1.0.3 is the specified version for end device registration.
Class C (Continuous) is the designated additional LoRaWAN class capability.
JoinEUI identifies the Join Server and facilitates initial communication between the end device and TTS.
DevEUI should be unique for each end device, even though the same JoinEUI can be used for multiple devices.
AppKey is a randomly generated key essential for the RS485-LN to connect to TTS.
Over the air activation (OTAA) is the default activation mode shown for LoRaWAN end devices.

warnings_notes: JoinEUI: This identifies the Join Server, which handles the initial communication between the end device and TTS. It is usually assigned by the device manufacturer, but it can also be set by the user.

If no JoinEUI is provided, a value of all zeros can be used if the Join Server supports it. It is possible to use the same JoinEUI for different end devices, but the DevEUI should be unique per end device.
AppKey: generate a random key (necessary for the RS485-LN to be able to connect to TTS)

2.2. RS485LN Configuration using Configuration Tool software

Figure 7: Diagram connection for RS485-LN configuration

2. Download the configuration tool from the next link Dragino RS485 Tool (https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fo/njwvfb6jr0bhi41ir7oot/APUxkgRkY_h6haHiZgQsiQ?rlkey=e49ir3zgj65tizsngghhztqs1&e=1&dl=0) and select the latest version.

key_facts: The document provides instructions for configuring an RS485LN device, including its network provisioning information.
The configuration process involves connecting the RS485LN device to a PC using a USB-TTL Adapter and a Program Cable.
The 'Dragino RS485 Tool' software, specifically version V1.3.1, is required for performing the configuration.
Users must select the correct COM port and set the baud rate to 9600 in the configuration tool.
The software allows for inputting JoinEUI, DevEUI, AppKey, and End device ID, and categorizing devices with labels.
The configuration tool enables sending poll commands and processing returned sensor data, either as byte blocks or discrete bytes.

  - Baudrate: 38400

10. Click on the RST button on the device, and now the RS485-LN should be connected to TTS.

2.3. Modbus RTU Commands Configuration with RS-485 Configuration Tool software
The RS485-LN accepts up to 15 commands per connected device. Each command allows reading one parameter from the Rheonics sensor. To read the registers and obtain the value desired, the command needed is composed of AA BB CC CC DD DD, where
    *   AA = Device ID
    *   BB = Function code
    *   CC CC = Start address of the register
    *   DD DD = Number of registers to read

For example, to read the viscosity, the command to send would be:
(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297459-modbus-input-registers)
AT+COMMAND1=01 04 00 28 00 02,1. The last 1 indicates that the CRC is added automatically. Refer to the Modbus Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297459-modbus-input-registers) to determine the rest of the parameters.

1. Open the Configuration Tool, connect to the corresponding COM, enter the default password, which is 123456, and click on Send. A message saying Correct Password should be returned. This allows sending the rest of AT Commands.

specifications.Example Modbus command for viscosity (pa: Device ID: 01, Function code: 04, Start address: 00 28, Number of registers: 00 02

key_facts: The device's APP EUI is confirmed successfully when the console returns 'recv:OK' after sending the AT+APPEUI command.
The APP KEY is configured by sending the 'AT+APPKEY=APPKEY' command, where 'APPKEY' is replaced with the specific key generated in TTS.
To verify successful upload of APPEUI and APPKEY, users should send 'AT+APPEUI=?' and 'AT+APPKEY=?' commands separately.
Clicking the RST button on the device after configuration connects the RS485-LN to TTS.
The RS485-LN accepts up to 15 Modbus RTU commands per connected device.
Each Modbus command allows reading one parameter from a Rheonics sensor.
Modbus commands are structured as 'AA BB CC CC DD DD', representing Device ID, Function code, Start address of the register, and Number of registers to read.
The trailing '1' in AT+COMMAND1 commands (e.g., AT+COMMAND1=01 04 00 28 00 02,1) signifies that the Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) is added automatically.
The default password for the Configuration Tool is 123456.
Entering the correct default password in the Configuration Tool and clicking Send should return a 'Correct Password' message, enabling the sending of further AT Commands.

chunks.page_summary: This page details how to send AT commands to a device to retrieve various sensor parameters and provides instructions for visualizing this sensor data on The Things Stack platform.
chunks.extracted_text:
recv:***** UpLinkCounter= 86 *****
recv:
TX on freq 868.500 MHz at DR 5
recv:RX on freq 869.525 MHz at DR 3
recv:TxDone
send:123456
recv:Correct Password
AT Command
123456
Send
Figure 14: Sending RS485-LN password

2. In AT Command write AT+COMMANDX=XX XX XX XX XX XX XX,1 with the values wanted. This command can go from AT+COMMAND1 to AT+COMMANDF.

recv:
Correct Password
send:AT+COMMAND1=01 04 00 28 00 02,1
recv:
recv:OK
AT Command
AT+COMMAND1=01 04 00 28 00 02,1
Send
Figure 15: Sending AT+COMMAND1 for viscosity

Here is the list of the commands for the 23 parameters. Change the first byte to the device ID required.

* Viscosity median = 01 04 00 28 00 02,1
* Density median = 01 04 00 30 00 02,1
* Temperature median = 01 04 00 38 00 02,1
* Kinematic viscosity = 01 04 00 40 00 02,1
* Density average = 01 04 00 48 00 02,1
* Viscosity raw = 01 04 00 50 00 02,1
* Density raw = 01 04 00 58 00 02,1
* Temperature raw = 01 04 00 60 00 02,1
* Resonant frequency = 01 04 00 68 00 02,1
* Compensated resonant frequency = 01 04 00 70 00 02,1
* Damping frequency = 01 04 00 78 00 02,1
* Coil temperature = 01 04 00 80 00 02,1
* Viscosity last good = 01 04 00 88 00 02,1
* Density last good = 01 04 00 90 00 02,1
* Mapped value (register 512) = 01 04 00 98 00 02,1
* Mapped value (register 514) = 01 04 00 A0 00 02,1
* Mapped value (register 516) = 01 04 00 A8 00 02,1
* Estimated temperature = 01 04 00 B0 00 02,1
* Temperature (PT1000 sensor) = 01 04 00 B8 00 02,1
* Calculated parameter from viscosity models = 01 04 00 C0 00 02,1
* Calculated parameter from density models = 01 04 00 C8 00 02,1
* Calculated parameter from concentration models = 01 04 00 D0 00 02,1
* Sensor cleanliness ratio = 01 04 00 D8 00 02,1

3. Sensor data visualization

1. Enter The Things Stack, and click on the end-device created.

Figure 16: End devices

2. Select the Payload formatters Tab.
key_facts: A password, '123456', is required to send commands to the device.
The page provides a list of 23 commands for retrieving various sensor parameters, including viscosity, density, and temperature.
The first byte of each command must be adjusted to match the device ID.
Sensor data can be visualized using 'The Things Stack' platform.
To visualize data, users should navigate to the created end-device in The Things Stack and select the 'Payload formatters Tab'.

chunks.page_summary: This page illustrates a Javascript function for decoding uplink payload data from sensors, mapping raw byte data to human-readable measurement parameters, and shows its configuration within an IoT platform's payload formatter settings.
key_facts: The `decodeUplink` function is a Javascript payload formatter used to interpret raw byte data from a sensor uplink.
It processes incoming byte streams to extract a device ID and then converts 4-byte segments into 32-bit floating-point numbers.

The function maps these decoded float values to a predefined list of 23 sensor measurement parameters, including various types of viscosity, density, temperature, frequency, and a sensor cleanliness ratio.
It includes logic to handle `NaN` (Not a Number) results from float conversion and formats valid numbers to six decimal places.
The output of the function is an object containing the decoded data, along with empty arrays for any warnings or errors.
The provided image shows this code configured as a 'Custom Javascript formatter' for an 'rs485-converter' device within an IoT platform's payload formatter settings.
The navigation path indicates this configuration is for an uplink message within the 'Payload formatters' section of an end device.

- End Device AT Commands and Downlink Command: https://wiki.dragino.com/xwiki/bin/view/Main/End%20Device%20AT%20Commands%20and%20Downlink%20Command/
key_facts: Users can visualize parameters obtained from Rheonics SME devices using a 'Live Data' tab within a web application interface.
The live data preview table displays timestamps, message types (e.g., 'Forward uplink data message', 'Successfully processed data message'), device addresses (DevAddr), and payload data.
Payload data includes measured parameters such as density_median, temperature_median, and viscosity_median.
The document provides references for Modbus RTU implementation, The Things Network Console, a Dragino RS485 Tool, and user manuals for RS485 to LoRaWAN converters and end device commands.
The system supports both uplink (data from device) and downlink (commands to device) messages.
